scenes.text: Introduction to Docker: Docker is an open-source platform that automates the deployment, scaling, and management of applications with containerization. It helps developers and system administrators build, ship, and run distributed applications.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker] --> B[Enhances deployment]; A --> C[Facilitates scaling]; A --> D[Manages applications];

scenes.text: Docker Architecture: Docker uses a client-server architecture. The Docker client communicates with the Docker daemon, which is responsible for building, running, and managing Docker containers.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Client] --> B[Docker Daemon]; B --> C[Builds]; B --> D[Runs]; B --> E[Manages Containers];

scenes.text: Docker Images: Docker images are read-only templates used to create containers. They include everything needed to run an application, ensuring consistency across environments.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Images] --> B[Read-only templates]; B --> C[Create Containers]; B --> D[Ensure Consistency];

scenes.text: Docker Containers: Containers are lightweight and portable encapsulations of environments where applications are executed. They are isolated but can share OS and resources.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Containers] --> B[Lightweight]; B --> C[Portable Environments]; B --> D[Isolated yet Share OS];

scenes.text: Docker Registry: Docker Registry is a service for storing and distributing Docker images. Docker Hub is a public registry, but private registries can be set up, too.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Registry] --> B[Stores Images]; B --> C[Distributes Images]; C --> D[Docker Hub]; C --> E[Private Registries];

scenes.text: Dockerfile: A Dockerfile is a text document that contains instructions for Docker to build an image. It automates repetitive tasks and ensures a consistent environment.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Dockerfile] --> B[Instructions to Build]; B --> C[Automates Tasks]; C --> D[Ensures Consistency];

scenes.text: Docker Compose: Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. It uses a YAML file to configure application services.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Compose] --> B[Defines Multi-container Apps]; B --> C[Uses YAML File]; C --> D[Configures Services];

scenes.text: Docker Networking: Docker provides various network drivers to connect containers. These include bridge, host, overlay, and macvlan drivers, allowing efficient communication.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Networking] --> B[Connects Containers]; B --> C[Bridge Driver]; C --> D[Host Driver]; D --> E[Overlay Driver]; E --> F[Macvlan Driver];

scenes.text: Docker Swarm: Docker Swarm is a native clustering and orchestration tool for Docker containers. It turns a group of Docker engines into a single virtual Docker engine.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Docker Swarm] --> B[Clustering Tool]; B --> C[Orchestrates Containers]; C --> D[Single Virtual Engine];

scenes.text: Conclusion and Benefits: Docker streamlines the development lifecycle, increases efficiency, and enhances collaboration. It solves the 'it works on my machine' problem.
scenes.image_prompt: graph TD; A[Conclusion] --> B[Streamlines Development]; B --> C[Increases Efficiency]; C --> D[Enhances Collaboration]; D --> E[Solves Compatibility Issues];
